original:
Gáttir allar áðr gangi fram
um skoðask skyli,
um skyggnast skyli,
því at óvíst er at vita hvar óvinir
sitja á fleti fyrir.

translations.bellows:
Within the gates before a man shall go,
(Full warily let him watch,)
Full long let him look about him;
For little he knows where a foe may lurk,
And sit in the seats within.
translations.bray:
At every door-way,
before one enters,
one should spy round,
one should pry round
for uncertain is the witting
that there be no foeman sitting,
within, before one on the floor
translations.petit:
Before advancing through all doorways,
one must look around,
one must peer around;
for one never knows for certain where enemies
are sitting on the boards.

original:
Gefendr heilir!
Gestr er inn kominn,
hvar skal sitja sjá? Mjök er bráðr
sá er á bröndum skal síns of freista frama.

translations.bellows:
Hail to the giver! a guest has come;
Where shall the stranger sit?
Swift shall he be who, with swords shall try
The proof of his might to make.
translations.bray:
Hail, you givers! a guest is come;
say! where shall he sit within?
Much pressed is he who fain on the hearth
would seek for warmth and weal.
translations.petit:
‘Hail to the givers!’ A guest has come in.
Where shall he sit?
He’s very anxious, the one who has
to test his fortune on the firewood.

original:
Elds er þörf þeims inn er kominn
ok á kné kalinn.
Matar ok váða er manni þörf,
þeim er hefr um fjall farit.

translations.bellows:
Fire he needs who with frozen knees
Has come from the cold without;
Food and clothes must the farer have,
The man from the mountains come.
translations.bray:
He has need of fire, who now is come,
numbed with cold to the knee;
food and clothing the wanderer craves
who has fared over the rimy fell.
translations.petit:
Fire is required for the one who has come in
and is chilled at the knee;
food and clothes are required for the man
who has travelled over mountains.

original:
Vatns er þörf þeim er til
verðar kemr, þerru ok þjóðlaðar,
góðs of æðis ef sér geta mætti
orðs ok endrþögu.

translations.bellows:
Water and towels and welcoming speech
Should he find who comes, to the feast;
If renown he would get, and again be greeted,
Wisely and well must he act.
translations.bray:
He craves for water, who comes for refreshment,
drying and friendly bidding,
marks of good will, fair fame if it is won,
and welcome once and again.
translations.petit:
Water is required for the one who comes to a meal,
a towel and a warm invitation,
a good disposition, if he can get it,
conversation and silence in return.

translations.bellows:
Wits must he have who wanders wide,
But all is easy at home;
At the witless man the wise shall wink
When among such men he sits.
translations.bray:
He has need of his wits who wanders wide,
anything simple will serve at home;
but a gazing-stock is the fool who sits
mid the wise, and nothing knows.

original:
At hyggjandi sinni skyli-t maðr hræsinn vera,
heldr gætinn at geði; þá er horskr ok þögull
kemr heimisgarða til, sjaldan verðr víti vörum,
því at óbrigðra vin fær maðr aldregi
en mannvit mikit.

translations.bray:
Let no man glory in the greatness of his mind,
but rather keep watch over his wits.
Cautious and silent let him enter a dwelling;
to the heedful comes seldom harm,
for none can find a more faithful friend
than the wealth of mother wit.

original:
Inn vari gestr, er til verðar kemr,
þunnu hljóði þegir, eyrum hlýðir,
en augum skoðar;
svá nýsisk fróðra hverr fyrir.

translations.bellows:
The knowing guest who goes to the feast,
In silent attention sits;
With his ears he hears, with his eyes he watches,
Thus wary are wise men all.

original:
Hinn er sæll, er sér of getr
lof ok líknstafi;
ódælla er við þat,
er maðr eiga skal annars brjóstum í.

translations.bellows:
Happy the one who wins for himself
Favor and praises fair;
Less safe by far is the wisdom found
That is hid in another's heart.
translations.bray:
Happy is he who wins for himself
fair fame and kindly words;
but uneasy is that which a man owns
while it lies in another's breast.
translations.petit:
Happy is that one who earns himself
praise and kindness-staves;
it’s less easy to deal with what one has to own
in the heart of another.

original:
Sá er sæll, er sjalfr of á
lof ok vit, meðan lifir;
því at ill ráð
hefr maðr oft þegit annars brjóstum ór.

translations.bellows:
Happy the man who has while he lives
Wisdom and praise as well,
For evil counsel a man full often
Has from another's heart.
translations.bray:
Happy is he who has in himself
praise and wisdom in life;
for often does a man ill counsel get
when it is born in another's breast.
translations.petit:
Happy is he who himself possesses
acclaim and intelligence while he lives;
for a man has often had bad advice
from another’s breast.

original:
Byrði betri berr-at maðr brautu at
en sé mannvit mikit; auði betra
þykkir þat í ókunnum stað;
slíkt er válaðs vera.

translations.bellows:
A better burden may no man bear
For wanderings wide than wisdom;
It is better than wealth on unknown ways,
And in grief a refuge it gives.
translations.bray:
A better burden can no man bear
on the way than his mother wit;
it is the refuge of the poor, and richer it seems
than wealth in a world untried.

original:
Byrði betri
berr-at maðr brautu at
en sé mannvit mikit; vegnest verra
vegr-a hann velli at en sé ofdrykkja öls.

translations.bellows:
A better burden may no man bear
For wanderings wide than wisdom;
Worse food for the journey he brings not afield
Than an over-drinking of ale.
translations.bray:
A better burden can no man bear
on the way than his mother wit:
and no worse provision can he carry with him
than too deep a draught of ale.
translations.petit:
One doesn’t bear a better burden on the road
than great common sense;
one can’t carry worse provisions through the country
than overindulgence in ale.

original:
Er-a svá gótt
sem gótt kveða
öl alda sona, því at færa veit,
er fleira drekkr síns til geðs gumi.

translations.bellows:
Less good there lies than most believe
In ale for mortal men;
For the more he drinks the less does man
Of his mind the mastery hold.
translations.bray:
Less good than they say for the sons of men
is the drinking often of ale:
for the more they drink, the less can they think
and keep a watch over their wits.

original:
Óminnishegri heitir
sá er yfir ölðrum þrumir,
hann stelr geði guma; þess fugls fjöðrum
ek fjötraðr vark í garði Gunnlaðar.

translations.bellows:
Over beer the bird of forgetfulness broods,
And steals the minds of men;
With the heron's feathers fettered I lay
And in Gunnloth's house was held.
translations.bray:
A bird of unmindfulness flutters over ale feasts,
wiling away men's wits:
with the feathers of that fowl I was fettered once
in the garths of Gunnlos below.

original:
Ölr ek varð,
varð ofrölvi
at ins fróða Fjalars; því er ölðr bazt,
at aftr of heimtir hverr sitt geð gumi.

translations.bellows:
Drunk I was, I was dead-drunk,
When with Fjalar wise I was;
It's the best of drinking if back one brings
His wisdom with him home.
translations.bray:
Drunk was I then, I was over drunk
in that crafty Jotun's court.
But best is an ale feast when man is able
to call back his wits at once.
translations.petit:
I got drunk, got extremely drunk,
at the house of wise Fjalarr;
in that case the best ale-feast is one where each man
gets his faculties back.

original:
Þagalt ok hugalt
skyli þjóðans barn
ok vígdjarft vera; glaðr ok reifr
skyli gumna hverr, unz sinn bíðr bana.

translations.bellows:
The son of a king shall be silent and wise,
And bold in battle as well;
Bravely and gladly a man shall go,
Till the day of his death is come.
translations.bray:
Silent and thoughtful and bold in strife
the prince's bairn should be.
Joyous and generous let each man show him
till he shall suffer death.
translations.petit:
A ruler’s child must be reserved and thoughtful
and brave in battle;
every man must be merry and cheerful
until he meets his death.

original:
Ósnjallr maðr hyggsk munu ey lifa,
ef hann við víg varask;
en elli gefr hánum engi frið,
þótt hánum geirar gefi.

translations.bellows:
The sluggard believes he shall live forever,
If the fight he faces not;
But age shall not grant him the gift of peace,
Though spears may spare his life.
translations.bray:
A coward believes he will ever live
if he keep him safe from strife:
but old age leaves him not long in peace
though spears may spare his life.
translations.petit:
A foolish man thinks he will live forever,
if he bewares of battle;
but old age will give him no peace,
even if spears give it to him.

original:
Kópir afglapi er til kynnis kemr,
þylsk hann um eða þrumir;
allt er senn, ef hann sylg of getr,
uppi er þá geð guma.

translations.bellows:
The fool is agape when he comes to the feast,
He stammers or else is still;
But soon if he gets a drink is it seen
What the mind of the man is like.
translations.bray:
A fool will gape when he goes to a friend,
and mumble only, or mope;
but pass him the ale cup and all in a moment
the mind of that man is shown.

original:
Sá einn veit er víða ratar
ok hefr fjölð of farit,
hverju geði stýrir gumna hverr,
sá er vitandi er vits.

translations.bellows:
He alone is aware who has wandered wide,
And far abroad has fared,
How great a mind is guided by him
That wealth of wisdom has.
translations.bray:
He knows alone who has wandered wide,
and far has fared on the way,
what manner of mind a man owns
who is wise of head and heart.
translations.petit:
Only the one who wanders widely
and has journeyed much
knows what disposition commands each man;
he’s knowing about the mind.

original:
Haldi-t maðr á keri,
drekki þó at hófi mjöð,
mæli þarft eða þegi,
ókynnis þess vár þik engi maðr,
at þú gangir snemma at sofa.

translations.bray:
Keep not the mead cup but drink your measure;
speak needful words or none:
none shall upbraid you for lack of breeding
if soon you seek'st your rest.

original:
Gráðugr halr, nema geðs viti,
etr sér aldrtrega;
oft fær hlægis, er með horskum kemr,
manni heimskum magi.

translations.bellows:
The greedy man, if his mind be vague,
Will eat till sick he is;
The vulgar man, when among the wise,
To scorn by his belly is brought.
translations.bray:
A greedy man, if he be not mindful,
eats to his own life's hurt:
often the belly of the fool will bring him to scorn
when he seeks the circle of the wise.
translations.petit:
A greedy man, unless he knows his inclination,
eats himself into life-sorrow;
the stomach often brings scorn on a foolish man,
when he comes among the wise.

original:
Hjarðir þat vitu, nær þær heim skulu,
ok ganga þá af grasi;
en ósviðr maðr kann ævagi
síns of mál maga.

translations.bellows:
The herds know well when home they shall fare,
And then from the grass they go;
But the foolish man his belly's measure
Shall never know aright.
translations.bray:
Herds know the hour of their going home
and turn them again from the grass;
but never is found a foolish man
who knows the measure of his maw.
translations.petit:
Herds know it, when they must go home,
and then go from the grass;
but a foolish man never knows
the measure of his maw.

original:
Vesall maðr ok illa skapi
hlær at hvívetna;
hittki hann veit, er hann vita þyrfti,
at hann er-a vamma vanr.

translations.bellows:
A paltry man and poor of mind
At all things ever mocks;
For never he knows, what he ought to know,
That he is not free from faults.
translations.bray:
The miserable man and evil minded
makes of all things mockery,
and knows not that which he best should know,
that he is not free from faults.
translations.petit:
A wretched man and one of wicked character
laughs at everything;
he doesn’t know what he needed to know,
that he’s not short of faults.

original:
Ósviðr maðr vakir um allar nætr
ok hyggr at hvívetna;
þá er móðr, er at morgni kemr,
allt er víl sem var.

translations.bellows:
The witless man is awake all night,
Thinking of many things;
Care-worn he is when the morning comes,
And his woe is just as it was.
translations.bray:
The unwise man is awake all night,
and ponders everything over;
when morning comes he is weary in mind,
and all is a burden as ever.
translations.petit:
A foolish man stays awake through all nights
and thinks about everything;
then he’s exhausted when morning arrives,
[and] all his trouble is as it was.

original:
Ósnotr maðr hyggr sér alla vera
viðhlæjendr vini;
hittki hann fiðr, þótt þeir um hann fár lesi,
ef hann með snotrum sitr.

translations.bellows:
The foolish man for friends all those
Who laugh at him will hold;
When among the wise he marks it not
Though hatred of him they speak.
translations.bray:
The unwise man weens all who smile
and flatter him are his friends,
nor notes how often they speak him ill
when he sits in the circle of the wise.
translations.petit:
A foolish man thinks all who laugh with him
are his friends;
he doesn’t notice, though they speak ill of him,
if he sits among the wise.

original:
Ósnotr maðr hyggr sér alla vera
viðhlæjendr vini;
þá þat finnr, er at þingi kemr,
at hann á formælendr fáa.

translations.bellows:
The foolish man for friends all those
Who laugh at him will hold;
But the truth when he comes to the council he learns,
That few in his favor will speak.
translations.bray:
The unwise man weens all who smile
and flatter him are his friends;
but when he shall come into court he shall find
there are few to defend his cause.
translations.petit:
A foolish man thinks all who laugh with him
are his friends;
then he finds it [out] when he comes to the assembly
that he has few speakers on his behalf.

original:
Ósnotr maðr þykkisk allt vita,
ef hann á sér í vá veru;
hittki hann veit,
hvat hann skal við kveða,
ef hans freista firar.

translations.bellows:
An ignorant man thinks that all he knows,
When he sits by himself in a corner;
But never what answer to make he knows,
When others with questions come.
translations.bray:
The unwise man thinks all to know
while he sits in a sheltered nook;
but he knows not one thing, what he shall answer,
if men shall put him to proof.
translations.petit:
A foolish man thinks he knows it all,
if he has a safe spot for himself in a corner;
he doesn’t know this — what he must say in response,
if people put him to the test.

translations.bellows:
A witless man, when he meets with men,
Had best in silence abide;
For no one shall find that nothing he knows,
If his mouth is not open too much.
(But a man knows not, if nothing he knows,
When his mouth has been open too much.)
translations.bray:
For the unwise man it is best to be mute
when he come amid the crowd,
for none is aware of his lack of wit
if he wastes not too many words;
for he who lacks wit shall never learn
though his words flow never so fast.

original:
Fróðr sá þykkisk, er fregna kann
ok segja it sama;
eyvitu leyna megu ýta synir,
því er gengr um guma.

translations.bellows:
Wise shall he seem who well can question,
And also answer well;
Nothing is concealed that men may say
Among the sons of men.
translations.bray:
Wise he is deemed who can question well,
and also answer back:
the sons of men can no secret make
of the tidings told in their midst.
translations.petit:
He who can ask and also answer
is considered wise;
the sons of men can conceal nothing
of what goes around men.

original:
Ærna mælir, sá er æva þegir,
staðlausu stafi;
hraðmælt tunga, nema haldendr eigi,
oft sér ógótt of gelr.

translations.bellows:
Often he speaks who never is still
With words that win no faith;
The babbling tongue, if a bridle it find not,
Often for itself sings ill.
translations.bray:
Too many unstable words are spoken
by him who never holds his peace;
the hasty tongue sings its own mishap
if it be not bridled in.
translations.petit:
He who is never silent speaks too many
staves of senselessness;
a fast-talking tongue, unless it has guards,
often invokes no good for itself.

original:
At augabragði skal-a maðr annan hafa,
þótt til kynnis komi;
margr þá fróðr þykkisk, ef hann freginn er-at
ok nái hann þurrfjallr þruma.

translations.bellows:
In mockery no one a man shall hold,
Although he fare to the feast;
Wise seems one often, if nothing he is asked,
And safely he sits dry-skinned.
translations.bray:
Let no man be held as a laughing-stock,
though he come as guest for a meal:
wise enough seem many while they sit dry-skinned
and are not put to proof.
translations.petit:
One shouldn’t make mock of another
when one visits an acquaintance;
many [a man] may then seem wise if he isn’t questioned
and he manages to sit quietly with his skin dry.

original:
Fróðr þykkisk, sá er flótta tekr,
gestr at gest hæðinn;
veit-a görla, sá er of verði glissir,
þótt hann með grömum glami.

translations.bellows:
Wise a guest holds it to take to his heels,
When mock of another he makes;
But little he knows who laughs at the feast,
Though he mocks in the midst of his foes.
translations.bray:
A guest thinks him witty who mocks at a guest
and runs from his wrath away;
but none can be sure who jests at a meal
that he makes not fun among foes.
translations.petit:
A guest scornful of a [fellow] guest
is thought wise — [that is,] the one who [in this situation] takes flight;
the one who’s sneering at a meal can’t be sure
whether he’s scoffing among enemies.

original:
Gumnar margir erusk gagnhollir,
en at virði vrekask;
aldar róg þat mun æ vera,
órir gestr við gest.

translations.bellows:
Friendly of mind are many men,
Till feasting they mock at their friends;
To mankind a bane must it ever be
When guests together strive.
translations.bray:
Often, though their hearts lean towards one another,
friends are divided at table;
ever the source of strife it will be,
that guest will anger guest.
translations.petit:
Many men are entirely friendly towards each other,
but abuse one another at a feast;
there will always be strife among men,
guest will fall out with guest.

original:
Árliga verðar skyli maðr oft fáa,
nema til kynnis komi:
str ok snópir, lætr sem solginn sé
ok kann fregna at fáu.

translations.bellows:
Often should one make an early meal,
Nor fasting come to the feast;
Else he sits and chews as if he would choke,
And little is able to ask.
translations.bray:
A man should take always his meals betimes
unless he visit a friend,
or he sits and mopes, and half famished seems,
and can ask or answer nothing.
translations.petit:
One should often have one’s meal early,
unless one visits an acquaintance;
[otherwise] one sits and craves food restlessly, acts as if famished,
and can ask about few things.

original:
Afhvarf mikit er til ills vinar,
þótt á brautu búi, en til góðs vinar
liggja gagnvegir,
þótt hann sé firr farinn.

translations.bellows:
Crooked and far is the road to a foe,
Though his house on the highway be;
But wide and straight is the way to a friend,
Though far away he fare.
translations.bray:
Long is the round to a false friend leading,
even if he dwell on the way:
but though far off fared, to a faithful friend
straight are the roads and short.
translations.petit:
It’s a very roundabout route to a bad friend’s house,
even if he lives on your way;
but straight roads lead to a good friend,
even if he’s gone further away.

original:
Ganga skal, skal-a gestr vera
ey í einum stað;
ljúfr verðr leiðr, ef lengi sitr
annars fletjum á.

translations.bellows:
Forth shall one go, nor stay as a guest
In a single spot forever;
Love becomes loathing if long one sits
By the hearth in another's home.
translations.bray:
A guest must depart again on his way,
nor stay in the same place ever;
if he bide too long on another's bench
the loved one soon becomes loathed.
translations.petit:
One must go, not always be
a guest in one place;
a loved man becomes loathed if he stays for long
on the floor of another.

original:
Bú er betra, þótt lítit sé,
halr er heima hverr;
þótt tvær geitr eigi ok taugreftan sal,
þat er þó betra en bæn.

translations.bellows:
Better a house, though a hut it be,
A man is master at home;
A pair of goats and a patched-up roof
Are better far than begging.

original:
Bú er betra, þótt lítit sé,
halr er heima hverr;
blóðugt er hjarta, þeim er biðja skal
sér í mál hvert matar.

translations.bellows:
Better a house, though a hut it be,
A man is master at home;
His heart is bleeding who needs must beg
When food he fain would have.
translations.bray:
One's own house is best, though small it may be,
each man is master at home;
with a bleeding heart will he beg, who must,
his meat at every meal.

original:
Vápnum sínum skal-a maðr velli á
feti ganga framar,
því at óvíst er at vita,
nær verðr á vegum úti geirs of þörf guma.

translations.bellows:
Away from his arms in the open field
A man should fare not a foot;
For never he knows when the need for a spear
Shall arise on the distant road.
translations.bray:
Let a man never stir on his road a step
without his weapons of war;
for unsure is the knowing when need shall arise
of a spear on the way without.
translations.petit:
One mustn’t go a foot from
one’s weapons in the field;
for, out on the roads, a man never knows for certain
when he’ll have need of his spear.

original:
Fannk-a ek mildan mann eða svá matar góðan,
at væri-t þiggja þegit,
eða síns féar
svági [glöggvan], at leið sé laun, ef þægi.

translations.bellows:
If wealth a man has won for himself,
Let him never suffer in need;
Often he saves for a foe what he plans for a friend,
For much goes worse than we wish.
translations.bray:
Let no man stint him and suffer need
of the wealth he has won in life;
often is saved for a foe what was meant for a friend,
and much goes worse than one weens.
translations.petit:
I haven’t encountered a man so kind or generous with food
that to accept was not [to be reckoned as having] accepted,
or so liberal with his wealth
that a reward would be loathed, if he could receive one.

translations.bellows:
None so free with gifts or food have I found
That gladly he took not a gift,
Nor one who so widely scattered his wealth
That of recompense hatred he had.
translations.bray:
Found none so noble or free with his food,
who was not gladdened with a gift,
nor one who gave of his gifts such store
but he loved reward, could he win it.

translations.bellows:
Friends shall gladden each other with arms and garments,
As each for himself can see;
Gift-givers' friendships are longest found,
If fair their fates may be.
translations.bray:
With raiment and arms shall friends gladden each other,
so has one proved oneself;
for friends last longest, if fate be fair
who give and give again.

original:
Vin sínum skal maðr vinr vera
ok gjalda gjöf við gjöf;
hlátr við hlátri skyli hölðar taka
en lausung við lygi.

translations.bellows:
To his friend a man a friend shall prove,
And gifts with gifts requite;
But men shall mocking with mockery answer,
And fraud with falsehood meet.
translations.bray:
To his friend a man should bear him as friend,
and gift for gift betow,
laughter for laughter let him exchange,
but leasing pay for a lie.
translations.petit:
One should be a friend to one’s friend,
and repay gift with gift;
men should requite laughter with laughter,
and lying with a lie.

original:
Vin sínum skal maðr vinr vera,
þeim ok þess vin;
en óvinar síns skyli engi maðr
vinar vinr vera.

translations.bellows:
To his friend a man a friend shall prove,
To him and the friend of his friend;
But never a man shall friendship make
With one of his foeman's friends.
translations.bray:
To his friend a man should bear him as friend,
to him and a friend of his;
but let him beware that he be not the friend
of one who is friend to his foe.
translations.petit:
One should be a friend to one’s friend,
to him and to his friend;
but no one should be a friend
of his enemy’s friend.

original:
Veiztu, ef þú vin átt, þann er þú vel trúir,
ok vill þú af hánum gótt geta,
geði skaltu við þann blanda
ok gjöfum skipta, fara at finna oft.

translations.bellows:
If a friend you have whom you fully will trust,
And good from him would get,
Your thoughts with his mingle, and gifts shall you make,
And fare to find him often.
translations.bray:
Have you got a friend that you trust well,
from whom you crave good?
Share your mind with him, gifts exchange with him,
fare to find him often.
translations.petit:
Know that if you have a friend whom you trust well,
and you want to get good from him,
you must mingle your mind with his and exchange gifts,
go to see him often.

original:
Ef þú átt annan, þanns þú illa trúir,
vildu af hánum þó gótt geta,
fagrt skaltu við þann mæla en flátt hyggja
ok gjalda lausung við lygi.

translations.bellows:
If another you have whom you hardly will trust,
Yet good from him would get,
You shall speak him fair, but falsely think,
And fraud with falsehood requite.
translations.bray:
But have you one whom you trust ill
yet from whom you crave good?
You shall speak him fair, but falsely think,
and leasing pay for a lie
translations.petit:
If you have another, whom you mistrust,
yet you want to get good from him,
you must speak fair with him but think false,
and repay lying with a lie.

original:
Það er enn of þann er þú illa trúir
ok þér er grunr at hans geði,
hlæja skaltu við þeim ok um hug mæla;
glík skulu gjöld gjöfum.

translations.bellows:
So is it with him whom you hardly will trust,
And whose mind you may not know;
Laugh with him may you, but speak not your mind,
Like gifts to his shall you give.
translations.bray:
Yet further of him whom you trusted ill,
and whose mind you misdoubtS;
though shall laugh with him but withhold your thought,
for gift with like gift should be paid.
translations.petit:
That’s also the case with the one whom you mistrust
and whose disposition you suspect:
you must laugh with him and mince the truth;
there should be like repayment for gifts.

original:
Ungr var ek forðum, fór ek einn saman,
þá varð ek villr vega;
auðigr þóttumk, er ek annan fann,
maðr er manns gaman.

translations.bellows:
Young was I once, and wandered alone,
And nothing of the road I knew;
Rich did I feel when a comrade I found,
For man is man's delight.
translations.bray:
Young was I once, I walked alone,
and bewildered seemed in the way;
then I found me another and rich I thought me,
for man is the joy of man.

original:
Mildir, fræknir menn bazt lifa,
sjaldan sút ala;
en ósnjallr maðr uggir hotvetna,
sýtir æ glöggr við gjöfum.

translations.bellows:
The lives of the brave and noble are best,
Sorrows they seldom feed;
But the coward fear of all things feels,
And not gladly the niggard gives.
translations.bray:
Most blest is he who lives free and bold
and nurses never a grief,
for the fearful man is dismayed by anything,
and the mean one mourns over giving.
translations.petit:
Generous, brave men live best,
they seldom nurse sorrow;
but a foolish man fears everything,
a niggard is always nervous about gifts.

translations.bellows:
My garments once in a field I gave
To a pair of carven poles;
Heroes they seemed when clothes they had,
But the naked man is nothing.

original:
Hrörnar þöll, sú er stendr þorpi á,
hlýr-at henni börkr né barr;
svá er maðr, sá er manngi ann.
Hvat skal hann lengi lifa?

translations.bellows:
On the hillside drear the fir-tree dies,
All bootless its needles and bark;
It is like a man whom no one loves,–
Why should his life be long?
translations.petit:
The fir-tree that stands in a farmstead withers,
neither bark nor needles protect it;
so it is with the man whom no one loves:
how is he to live for long?

original:
Eldi heitari brennr
með illum vinum friðr fimm daga,
en þá sloknar, er inn sétti kemr,
ok versnar allr vinskapr.

translations.bellows:
Hotter than fire between false friends
Does friendship five days burn;
When the sixth day comes the fire cools,
And ended is all the love.
translations.bray:
Fiercer than fire among ill friends
for five days love will burn;
but anon it is quenched, when the sixth day comes,
and all friendship soon is spoiled.
translations.petit:
Love burns hotter than fire for five days
among false friends,
but then dies down when the sixth comes,
and all friendship worsens.

original:
Mikit eitt skal-a manni gefa;
oft kaupir sér í litlu lof,
með halfum hleif ok með höllu keri
fekk ek mér félaga.

translations.bellows:
No great thing needs a man to give,
Often little will purchase praise;
With half a loaf and a half-filled cup
A friend full fast I made.
translations.bray:
Not great things alone must one give to another,
praise often is earned for nothing;
with half a loaf and a tilted bowl
I have found me many a friend.
translations.petit:
One shouldn’t give a man only great things,
one can often buy oneself love with little;
with half a loaf and with a tilted cup
I got myself a comrade.

translations.bellows:
A little sand has a little sea,
And small are the minds of men;
Though all men are not equal in wisdom,
Yet half-wise only are all.
translations.bray:
Little the sand if little the seas,
little are minds of men,
for never in the world were all equally wise,
it is shared by the fools and the sage.

translations.bellows:
A measure of wisdom each man shall have,
But never too much let him know;
The fairest lives do those men live
Whose wisdom wide has grown.
translations.petit:
Every man should be moderately wise,
let him never be too wise;
those people who know just enough
live the pleasantest lives.

original:
Meðalsnotr skyli manna hverr,
æva til snotr sé;
því at snotrs manns hjarta
verðr sjaldan glatt,
ef sá er alsnotr, er á.

translations.bellows:
A measure of wisdom each man shall have,
But never too much let him know;
For the wise man's heart is seldom happy,
If wisdom too great he has won.

original:
Meðalsnotr skyli manna hverr,
æva til snotr sé;
örlög sín viti engi fyrir,
þeim er sorgalausastr sefi.

translations.bellows:
A measure of wisdom each man shall have,
But never too much let him know;
Let no man the fate before him see,
For so is he freest from sorrow.
translations.bray:
Wise in measure should each man be,
but never let him wax too wise:
who does not look forward to learn his fate
unburdened heart will bear.

original:
Brandr af brandi brenn, unz brunninn er,
funi kveikisk af funa;
maðr af manni verðr at máli kuðr,
en til dælskr af dul.

translations.bellows:
A brand from a brand is kindled and burned,
And fire from fire begotten;
And man by his speech is known to men,
And the stupid by their stillness.
translations.bray:
Brand kindles from brand till it be burned,
spark is kindled from spark,
man unfold him by speech with man,
but grows over secret through silence.
translations.petit:
Brand catches fire from brand, until it is burnt,
flame is kindled from flame;
man becomes wise from man by speech,
and too foolish from reticence.

original:
Ár skal rísa, sá er annars vill
fé eða fjör hafa;
sjaldan liggjandi ulfr
lær of getr né sofandi maðr sigr.

translations.bellows:
He must early go forth who fain the blood
Or the goods of another would get;
The wolf that lies idle shall win little meat,
Or the sleeping man success.
translations.bray:
He must rise betimes who fain of another
or life or wealth would win;
scarce falls the prey to sleeping wolves,
or to slumberers victory in strife.
translations.petit:
He must rise early, the one who wants to have
another’s wealth or life;
a lounging wolf seldom gets the ham,
nor a sleeping man the victory.

original:
Ár skal rísa, sá er á yrkjendr fáa,
ok ganga síns verka á vit;
margt of dvelr, þann er um morgin sefr,
hálfr er auðr und hvötum.

translations.bellows:
He must early go forth whose workers are few,
Himself his work to seek;
Much remains undone for the morning-sleeper,
For the swift is wealth half won.
translations.bray:
He must rise betimes who has few to serve him,
and see to his work himself;
who sleeps at morning is hindered much,
to the keen is wealth half-won.
translations.petit:
He must rise early, the one who has few workmen,
and go to inspect his work;
many things will hinder the one who sleeps through the morning,
wealth is half in the hands of the willing.

original:
Þurra skíða ok þakinna næfra,
þess kann maðr mjöt, þess viðar,
er vinnask megi mál ok misseri.

translations.bellows:
Of seasoned shingles and strips of bark
For the thatch let one know his need,
And how much of wood he must have for a month,
Or in half a year he will use.
translations.bray:
Of dry logs saved and roof-bark stored
a man can know the measure,
of fire-wood too which should last him out
quarter and half years to come.
translations.petit:
Of dry sticks and bark for roofing,
a man knows his measure of this,
and of the wood that can suffice
for three months and for six.

original:
Þveginn ok mettr
ríði maðr þingi at,
þótt hann sé-t væddr til vel;
skúa ok bróka skammisk engi maðr
né hests in heldr, þótt hann hafi-t góðan

translations.bellows:
Washed and fed to the council fare,
But care not too much for your clothes;
Let none be ashamed of his shoes and hose,
Less still of the steed he rides,
(Though poor be the horse he has.)
translations.bray:
Fed and washed should one ride to court
though in garments none too new;
you shall not shame you for shoes or breeks,
nor yet for a sorry steed.
translations.petit:
Washed and fed let a man ride to the assembly,
even if he isn’t too well attired;
of his shoes and breeches let no man be ashamed,
or of his horse either, even if he hasn’t a good one.

original:
Snapir ok gnapir, er til sævar kemr,
örn á aldinn mar;
svá er maðr, er með mörgum kemr
ok á formælendr fáa.

translations.bellows:
When the eagle comes to the ancient sea,
He snaps and hangs his head;
So is a man in the midst of a throng,
Who few to speak for him finds.
translations.bray:
As the eagle who comes to the ocean shore,
Sniffs and hangs her head,
Dumfounded is he who finds at the Thing
No supporters to plead his case.
translations.petit:
When an eagle comes to the sea it snaps
and stretches at the ancient ocean;
so is a man who comes among many
and has few speakers on his behalf.

original:
Fregna ok segja skal fróðra hverr,
sá er vill heitinn horskr;
einn vita né annarr skal,
þjóð veit, ef þrír ro.

translations.bellows:
To question and answer must all be ready
Who wish to be known as wise;
Tell one your thoughts, but beware of two,–
All know what is known to three.
translations.bray:
Each man who is wise and would wise be called
must ask and answer aright.
Let one know your secret, but never a second, -
if three a thousand shall know.
translations.petit:
Every wise man who wants to be called sensible
must ask and answer;
one man should know, but not a second,
the nation will know, if there are three.

original:
Ríki sitt skyli ráðsnotra
hverr í hófi hafa;
þá hann þat finnr, er með fræknum kemr
at engi er einna hvatastr.

translations.bellows:
The man who is prudent a measured use
Of the might he has will make;
He finds when among the brave he fares
That the boldest he may not be.
translations.bray:
A wise counselled man will be mild in bearing
and use his might in measure,
lest when he come his fierce foes among
he find others fiercer than he.
translations.petit:
Every man wise in counsel should keep
his power in check;
he will find it [out], when he comes among the brave,
that no one is boldest of all.

original:
-- -- -- --
-- -- -- --
orða þeira, er maðr öðrum segir
oft hann gjöld of getr.

translations.bellows:
. . .
Often for the words that to others one speaks
He will get but an evil gift.
translations.bray:
Each man should be watcfhful and wary in speech,
and slow to put faith in a friend.
for the words which one to another speaks
he may wind reward of ill.
translations.petit:
For the words that one says to another,
one often receives repayment.

original:
Mikilsti snemma kom ek í marga staði,
en til síð í suma;
öl var drukkit, sumt var ólagat,
sjaldan hittir leiðr í líð.

translations.bray:
At many a feast I was far too late,
and much too soon at some;
drunk was the ale or yet unserved:
never hits he the joint who is hated.
translations.petit:
Much too early I came to many places,
and too late to some;
the ale had been drunk, [or] some was unbrewed,
the hated one seldom hits the mark.

original:
Hér ok hvar myndi mér heim of boðit,
ef þyrftak at málungi mat,
eða tvau lær hengi at ins tryggva vinar,
þars ek hafða eitt etit.

translations.bellows:
To their homes men would bid me hither and yon,
If at meal-time I needed no meat,
Or would hang two hams in my true friend's house,
Where only one I had eaten.
translations.bray:
Here and there to a home I had haply been asked
had I needed no meat at my meals,
or were two hams left hanging in the house of that friend
where I had partaken of one.
translations.petit:
Here and there I would be invited home
if I didn’t need food at meal-times;
or two hams might hang in the true friend’s house
when I had [already] eaten one.

original:
Eldr er beztr með ýta sonum
ok sólar sýn, heilyndi sitt,
ef maðr hafa náir,
án við löst at lifa.

translations.bellows:
Fire for men is the fairest gift,
And power to see the sun;
Health as well, if a man may have it,
And a life not stained with sin.
translations.bray:
Most dear is fire to the sons of men,
most sweet the sight of the sun;
good is health if one can but keep it,
and to live a life without shame.
translations.petit:
Fire is best among the sons of men,
and the sight of the sun,
his health, if a man can keep it,
to live without disability.

original:
Er-at maðr alls vesall,
þótt hann sé illa heill;
sumr er af sonum sæll, sumr af frændum,
sumr af fé ærnu, sumr af verkum vel.

translations.bellows:
All wretched is no man, though never so sick;
Some from their sons have joy,
Some win it from kinsmen, and some from their wealth,
And some from worthy works.
translations.bray:
Not reft of all is he who is ill,
for some are blest in their bairns,
some in their kin and some in their wealth,
and some in working well.
translations.petit:
A man isn’t entirely wretched, even if he’s in bad health:
one man is blessed in sons,
one in kinsmen, one in sufficient wealth,
one is well-[blessed] in deeds.

original:
Betra er lifðum en sé ólifðum,
ey getr kvikr kú;
eld sá ek upp brenna auðgum manni fyrir,
en úti var dauðr fyr durum.

translations.bellows:
It is better to live than to lie a corpse,
The live man catches the cow;
I saw flames rise for the rich man's pyre,
And before his door he lay dead.
translations.bray:
More blest are the living than the lifeless,
it is the living who come by the cow;
I saw the hearth-fire burn in the rich man's hall
and himself lying dead at the door.
translations.petit:
It’s better for the living than the unliving,
the live man always gets the cow;
I saw fire blaze up before a rich man,
but death was outside the door.

original:
Haltr ríðr hrossi, hjörð rekr handar vanr,
daufr vegr ok dugir,
blindr er betri en brenndr séi,
nýtr manngi nás.

translations.bellows:
The lame rides a horse, the handless is herdsman,
The deaf in battle is bold;
The blind man is better than one that is burned,
No good can come of a corpse.
translations.bray:
The lame can ride rose, the handless drive cattle,
the deaf one can fight and prevail,
it is happier for the blind than for him on the bale-fire,
but no man has care for a corpse.

original:
Sonr er betri, þótt sé síð of alinn
eftir genginn guma;
sjaldan bautarsteinar standa brautu nær,
nema reisi niðr at nið.

translations.bellows:
A son is better, though late he be born,
And his father to death have fared;
Memory-stones seldom stand by the road
Save when kinsman honors his kin.
translations.bray:
Best have a son though he be late born
and before him the father be dead:
seldom are stones raised on the wayside
save by kinsmen to kinsmen.
translations.petit:
A son is better, even if he’s born late,
after the father has passed on; 
memorial stones seldom stand by the road
unless kinsman raises them for kinsman.

original:
Tveir ro eins herjar, tunga er höfuðs bani;
er mér í heðin hvern handar væni.

translations.bellows:
Two make a battle, the tongue slays the head;
In each furry coat a fist I look for.
translations.bray:
Two are hosts against one, the tongue is the head's bane,
'neath a rough hide a hand may be hid.
translations.petit:
Two are the destroyers of one; the tongue is the head’s slayer;
I expect a hand inside every fur cloak.

translations.bellows:
He welcomes the night whose fare is enough,
(Short are the yards of a ship,)
Uneasy are autumn nights;
Full often does the weather change in a week,
And more in a month's time.
translations.bray:
He is glad at night fall who knows of his lodging,
short is the ship's berth,
and changeful the autumn night,
much veers the wind before the fifth day
and blows round yet more in a month.

original:
Veit-a hinn, er vettki veit,
margr verðr af aurum api;
maðr er auðigr, annar óauðigr,
skyli-t þann vítka váar.

translations.bellows:
A man knows not, if nothing he knows,
That gold often apes begets;
One man is wealthy and one is poor,
Yet scorn for him none should know.
translations.bray:
He who learns nothing will never know
how one is the fool of another, CHECK
for if one be rich another is poor
and for that should bear no blame.
translations.petit:
He who knows nothing doesn’t know it:
many a man becomes an ape through money;
one man is wealthy, another unwealthy,
one shouldn’t blame him for his misfortune.

original:
Deyr fé, deyja frændr,
deyr sjalfr it sama,
en orðstírr deyr aldregi,
hveim er sér góðan getr.

translations.bellows:
Cattle die, and kinsmen die,
And so one dies one's self;
But a noble name will never die,
If good renown one gets.
translations.bray:
Cattle die and kinsmen die,
yourself too soon must die,
but one thing never, I think, will die, -
fair fame of one who has earned.
translations.petit:
Cattle die, kinsmen die,
one dies oneself the same;
but reputation never dies
for whoever gets himself a good one.

original:
Deyr fé, deyja frændr,
deyr sjalfr it sama,
ek veit einn, at aldrei deyr:
dómr um dauðan hvern.

translations.bellows:
Cattle die, and kinsmen die,
And so one dies one's self;
One thing now that never dies,
The fame of a dead man's deeds.
translations.bray:
Cattle die and kinsmen die,
yourself too soon must die,
but one thing will never die, I think -
the doom on each one dead.
translations.petit:
Cattle die, kinsmen die,
one dies oneself the same;
I know one thing that never dies:
the renown of every dead man.

original:
Fullar grindr sá ek fyr Fitjungs sonum,
nú bera þeir vánar völ;
svá er auðr sem augabragð,
hann er valtastr vina.
academic_note: Scholarly Note: Bellows/Bray move this stanza earlier in their editions.

translations.bellows:
Among Fitjung's sons saw I well-stocked folds,–
Now bear they the beggar's staff;
Wealth is as swift as a winking eye,
Of friends the falsest it is.
translations.bray:
Full-stocked folds had the Fatling's sons,
who bear now a beggar's staff:
brief is wealth, as the winking of an eye,
most faithless ever of friends.

original:
Ósnotr maðr,
ef eignask getr
fé eða fljóðs munuð,
metnaðr hánum þróask,
en mannvit aldregi,
fram gengr hann drjúgt í dul.

translations.bellows:
Certain is that which is sought from runes,
That the gods so great have made,
And the Master-Poet painted;
. . .
the race of gods:
Silence is safest and best.
translations.bray:
All will prove true that you ask of runes -
those that are come from the gods,
which the high Powers wrought, and which Odin painted:
then silence is surely best.
translations.petit:
If a foolish man gets hold of wealth
or a woman’s love,
his pride increases, but his intelligence never;
forward he goes, headlong into folly.

original:
Þat er þá reynt, er þú að rúnum spyrr
inum reginkunnum, þeim er gerðu ginnregin
ok fáði fimbulþulr, þá hefir hann bazt, ef hann þegir.

translations.bellows:
An unwise man, if a maiden's love
Or wealth he chances to win,
His pride will wax, but his wisdom never,
Straight forward he fares in conceit.
* * *
translations.bray:
If haply a fool should find for himself
wealth or a woman's love,
pride waxes in him but wisdom never
and onward he fares in his folly.
translations.petit:
It’s proven then, when you enquire of the runes,
those of divine descent,
those which mighty powers made
and Fimbulþulr coloured;
he does best then if he keeps quiet.

original:
At kveldi skal dag leyfa,
konu, er brennd er, mæki, er reyndr er,
mey, er gefin er, ís, er yfir kemr,
öl, er drukkit er.

translations.bellows:
Give praise to the day at evening, to a woman on her pyre,
To a weapon which is tried, to a maid at wed lock,
To ice when it is crossed, to ale that is drunk.
translations.bray:
Praise day at even, a wife when dead,
a weapon when tried, a maid when married,
ice when it is crossed, and ale when it is drunk.
translations.petit:
One should praise a day in the evening, a woman when she’s been burned,
a sword when it’s been proven, a girl when she’s been given in marriage,
ice when it’s been crossed, ale when it’s been drunk.

original:
Í vindi skal við höggva,
veðri á sjó róa, myrkri við man spjalla,
mörg eru dags augu; á skip skal skriðar orka,
en á skjöld til hlífar, mæki höggs,
en mey til kossa.

translations.bray:
Hew wood in wind, sail the seas in a breeze,
woo a maid in the dark, - for day's eyes are many, -
work a ship for its gliding, a shield for its shelter,
a sword for its striking, a maid for her kiss;
translations.petit:
One should fell a tree in a wind, row to sea in fine weather,
chat with a girl in the dark; many are the eyes of day;
one should look to a ship for speed, and to a shield for protection,
a sword for a blow, and a girl for kisses.

original:
Við eld skal öl drekka,
en á ísi skríða, magran mar kaupa,
en mæki saurgan, heima hest feita,
en hund á búi.

translations.bellows:
By the fire drink ale, over ice go on skates;
Buy a steed that is lean, and a sword when tarnished,
The horse at home fatten, the hound in your dwelling.
* * *

original:
Meyjar orðum skyli manngi trúa
né því, er kveðr kona, því at á hverfanda hvéli
váru þeim hjörtu sköpuð, brigð í brjóst of lagið.

translations.bellows:
A man shall trust not the oath of a maid,
Nor the word a woman speaks;
For their hearts on a whirling wheel were fashioned,
And fickle their breasts were formed.
translations.bray:
The speech of a maiden should no man trust
nor the words which a woman says;
for their hearts were shaped on a whirling wheel
and falsehood fixed in their breasts.
translations.petit:
No one should trust a girl’s words,
or what a woman says,
because their hearts were shaped on a whirling wheel,
fickleness was laid in their breast.

original:
Brestanda boga, brennanda loga,
gínanda ulfi, galandi kráku,
rýtanda svíni, rótlausum viði,
vaxanda vági, vellanda katli,

translations.bellows:
In a breaking bow or a burning flame,
A ravening wolf or a croaking raven,
In a grunting boar, a tree with roots broken,
In billowy seas or a bubbling kettle,
translations.bray:
Breaking bow, or flaring flame,
ravening wolf, or croaking raven,
routing swine, or rootless tree,
waxing wave, or seething cauldron,
translations.petit:
In a breaking bow, in a burning flame,
in a gaping wolf, in a cawing crow,
in a grunting boar, in a rootless tree,
in a rising wave, in a boiling cauldron,

original:
Fljúganda fleini, fallandi báru,
ísi einnættum, ormi hringlegnum,
brúðar beðmálum eða brotnu sverði,
bjarnar leiki eða barni konungs.

translations.bellows:
In a flying arrow or falling waters,
In ice new formed or the serpent's folds,
In a bride's bed-speech or a broken sword,
In the sport of bears or in sons of kings,
translations.bray:
flying arrows, or falling billow,
ice of a night time, coiling adder,
woman's bed-talk, or broken blade,
play of bears or a prince's child,
translations.petit:
in a flying shaft, in a falling wave,
in one-night-old ice, in a coiled snake,
in a bride’s bed-talk or in a broken sword,
in a bear’s play or in a king’s child,

original:
Sjúkum kalfi, sjalfráða þræli,
völu vilmæli, val nýfelldum.

translations.bellows:
In a calf that is sick or a stubborn thrall,
A flattering witch or a foe new slain.
"In a light, clear sky or a laughing throng,
In the bowl of a dog or a harlot's grief!"
translations.bray:
sickly calf or self-willed thrall,
witch's flattery, new-slain foe,
translations.petit:
in a sick calf, in a self-willed slave,
in a seeress’s satisfying words, in a newly-felled man,

original:
Akri ársánum trúi engi maðr
né til snemma syni, - veðr ræðr akri.
en vit syni;
hætt er þeira hvárt.

translations.bellows:
In a brother's slayer, if you meet him abroad,
In a half-burned house, in a horse full swift–
One leg is hurt and the horse is useless–
None had ever such faith as to trust in them all.
translations.bray:
Brother's slayer, though seen on the highway,
half burned house, or horse too swift -
be never so trustful as these to trust.
translations.petit:
in a field sown early, let no one have faith,
nor in a son too soon —
weather rules the field, and sense the son;
each of them is at risk —

original:
Bróðurbana sínum þótt á brautu mæti,
húsi hálfbrunnu, hesti alskjótum,
- þá er jór ónýtr, ef einn fótr brotnar -,
verði-t maðr svá tryggr at þessu trúi öllu.

translations.bellows:
Hope not too surely for early harvest,
Nor trust too soon in your son;
The field needs good weather, the son needs wisdom,
And often is either denied.
translations.bray:
Let none put faith in the first sown fruit
nor yet in his son too soon;
whim rules the child, and weather the field,
each is open to chance.
translations.petit:
in one’s brother’s slayer, if one should meet him on the road,
in a half-burned house, in a very fast horse —
a horse is useless if it breaks one leg —
let no man become so trusting that he puts faith in all these!

original:
Svá er friðr kvenna, þeira er flátt hyggja,
sem aki jó óbryddum á ísi hálum,
teitum, tvévetrum ok sé tamr illa,
eða í byr óðum beiti stjórnlausu,
eða skyli haltr henda hrein í þáfjalli.

translations.bellows:
The love of women fickle of will
Is like starting over ice with a steed unshod,
A two-year-old restive and little tamed,
Or steering a rudderless ship in a storm,
Or, lame, hunting reindeer on slippery rocks.
* * *
translations.bray:
Like the love of women whose thoughts are lies
is the driving un-roughshod over slippery ice
of a two year old, ill-tamed and gay;
or in a wild wind steering a helmless ship,
or the lame catching reindeer in the rime-thawed fell.
translations.petit:
Loving women, those who think deceitful thoughts,
is like driving a horse without spikes on slippery ice,
a frisky two-year-old and ill-trained,
or like tacking a rudderless ship against a raging wind,
or having, as a limping man, to lay hands on a reindeer on a thawing mountain.

original:
Bert ek nú mæli, því at ek bæði veit,
brigðr er karla hugr konum;
þá vér fegrst mælum, er vér flást hyggjum:
þat tælir horska hugi.

translations.bellows:
Clear now will I speak, for I know them both,
Men false to women are found;
When fairest we speak, then falsest we think,
Against wisdom we work with deceit.
translations.petit:
I shall now speak plainly, for I know both —
men’s feelings for women are fickle;
when we speak most sweetly, we think most deceitfully —
that deludes wise minds.

original:
Fagrt skal mæla ok fé bjóða,
sá er vill fljóðs ást fá,
líki leyfa ins ljósa mans,
sá fær, er fríar.

translations.bellows:
Soft words shall he speak and wealth shall he offer
Who longs for a maiden's love,
And the beauty praise of the maiden bright;
He wins whose wooing is best.
translations.bray:
Let him speak soft words and offer wealth
who longs for a woman's love,
praise the shape of the shining maid -
he wins who thus wooes.
translations.petit:
He must speak sweetly and offer wealth,
the one who wants to win a woman’s love,
praise the radiant girl’s body,
he who woos wins.

original:
Ástar firna skyli engi maðr
annan aldregi;
oft fá á horskan, er á heimskan né fá,
lostfagrir litir.

translations.bellows:
Fault for loving let no man find
Ever with any other;
Often the wise are fettered, where fools go free,
By beauty that breeds desire.
translations.bray:
Never a whit should one blame another
whom love has brought into bonds:
often a witching form will fetch the wise
which does not hold the heart of fools.
translations.petit:
No one should ever find fault
with another over love;
often ravishingly fair looks enthral a wise man,
when they don’t enthral a fool.

original:
Eyvitar firna er maðr annan skal,
þess er um margan gengr guma;
heimska ór horskum gerir hölða sonu
sá inn máttki munr.

translations.bellows:
Fault with another let no man find
For what touches many a man;
Wise men often into witless fools
Are made by mighty love.
translations.bray:
Never a whit should one blame another
for a folly which many befalls;
the might of love makes sons of men
into fools who once were wise.
translations.petit:
One should find fault with another
for nothing that happens to many a man;
that mighty desire makes dolts
out of wise sons of men.

original:
Hugr einn þat veit, er býr hjarta nær,
einn er hann sér of sefa;
öng er sótt verri hveim snotrum manni
en sér engu at una.

translations.bellows:
The head alone knows what dwells near the heart,
A man knows his mind alone;
No sickness is worse to one who is wise
Than to lack the longed-for joy.
translations.bray:
The mind knows alone what is nearest the heart
and sees where the soul is turned:
no sickness seems to the wise so sore
as in nothing to know content.
translations.petit:
The mind alone knows that which lives near the heart,
he is alone with his thoughts;
no sickness is worse for any wise man
than to be content with nothing.

translations.bellows:
This found I myself, when I sat in the reeds,
And long my love awaited;
As my life the maiden wise I loved,
Yet her I never had.
translations.bray:
This once I felt when I sat without
in the reeds, and looked for my love;
body and soul of me was that sweet maiden
yet never I won her as wife.
translations.petit:
I proved that when I sat in the reeds
and waited for my love;
the wise girl was flesh and heart to me,
yet I have her none the more for that.

original:
Billings mey ek fann beðjum á
sólhvíta sofa;
jarls ynði þótti mér ekki vera
nema við þat lík at lifa.

translations.bellows:
Billing's daughter I found on her bed,
In slumber bright as the sun;
Empty appeared an earl's estate
Without that form so fair.
translations.bray:
Billing's daughter I found on her bed,
fairer than sunlight sleeping,
and the sweets of lordship seemed to me nothing,
save I lived with that lovely form.
translations.petit:
I found Billingr’s daughter in bed,
sleeping, sun-white;
an earl’s delight seemed as nothing to me,
unless I could live with that body.

original:
"Auk nær aftni skaltu, Óðinn, koma,
ef þú vilt þér mæla man;
allt eru ósköp, nema einir viti
slíkan löst saman."

translations.bellows:
"Othin, again at evening come,
If a woman you would win;
Evil it were if others than we
Should know of such a sin."
translations.bray:
"Yet nearer evening come you, Odin,
if you will woo a maiden:
all were undone save two knew alone
such a secret deed of shame."
translations.petit:
‘You must come again towards evening, Óðinn,
if you want to win the girl with words;
all will be undone, unless we alone
know of such shame together!’

original:
Aftr ek hvarf ok unna þóttumk
vísum vilja frá;
hitt ek hugða, at ek hafa mynda
geð hennar allt ok gaman.

translations.bellows:
Away I hastened, hoping for joy,
And careless of counsel wise;
Well I believed that soon I should win
Measureless joy with the maid.
translations.bray:
So away I turned from my wise intent,
and deemed my joy assured,
for all her liking and all her love
I thought that I yet should win.
translations.petit:
Back I turned and thought myself in love,
from certain delight;
I thought this, that I would have
her whole heart and love-play.

original:
Svá kom ek næst, at in nýta var
vígdrótt öll of vakin með brennandum ljósum
ok bornum viði, svá var mér vílstígr of vitaðr.

translations.bellows:
So came I next when night it was,
The warriors all were awake;
With burning lights and waving brands
I learned my luckess way.
translations.bray:
When I came before long the war troop bold
were watching and waking all:
with burning brands and torches borne
they showed me my sorrowful way.
translations.petit:
Thus [it was when] I came next,
that the able warband was all awake;
with burning lights and raised wood,
thus was a misery-path appointed for me.

original:
Auk nær morgni, er ek var enn of kominn,
þá var saldrótt of sofin;
grey eitt ek þá fann innar góðu konu
bundit beðjum á.

translations.bellows:
At morning then, when once more I came,
And all were sleeping still,
A dog found in the fair one's place,
Bound there upon her bed.
translations.bray:
Yet nearer morning I went, once more, -
the housefolk slept in the hall,
but soon I found a barking dog
tied fast to that fair maid's couch.
translations.petit:
And toward morning, when I had come again,
the hall-guard was asleep;
then I found one of the good woman’s bitches
bound on the bed.

translations.bellows:
Many fair maids, if a man but tries them,
False to a lover are found;
That did I learn when I longed to gain
With wiles the maiden wise;
Foul scorn was my meed from the crafty maid,
And nothing from the woman I won.
* * *
translations.bray:
Many a sweet maid when one knows her mind
is fickle found towards men:
I proved it well when that prudent lass
I sought to lead astray:
shrewd maid, she sought me with every insult
and I won therewith no wife.
translations.petit:
Many a good girl, if one gets to know her fully,
is fickle of mind towards men,
I proved that when I sought to seduce
the counsel-wise woman into deceit;
the wise girl attacked me with every insult,
and I had nothing of this woman.

original:
Heima glaðr gumi ok við gesti reifr,
sviðr skal um sig vera, minnigr ok málugr,
ef hann vill margfróðr vera,
oft skal góðs geta;
fimbulfambi heitir, sá er fátt kann segja,
þat er ósnotrs aðal.

translations.bray:
In your home be joyous and generous to guests
discreet shall you be in your bearing,
mindful and talkative, would you gain wisdom,
often making me mention of good.
He is "Simpleton" named who has nothing to say,
for such is the fashion of fools.

original:
Inn aldna jötun ek sótta,
nú em ek aftr of kominn: fátt gat ek þegjandi þar;
mörgum orðum mælta ek í minn frama
í Suttungs sölum.

translations.bellows:
I found the old giant, now back have I fared,
Small gain from silence I got;
Full many a word, my will to get,
I spoke in Suttung's hall.
translations.bray:
I sought that old Jotun, now safe am I back,
little served my silence there;
but whispering many soft speeches I won
my desire in Suttung's halls.

original:
Gunnlöð mér of gaf gullnum stóli á
drykk ins dýra mjaðar;
ill iðgjöld lét ek hana eftir hafa
síns ins heila hugar, síns ins svára sefa.

translations.bellows:
The mouth of Rati made room for my passage,
And space in the stone he gnawed; Above and below the giants' paths lay,
So rashly I risked my head.

original:
Rata munn létumk
rúms of fá ok um grjót gnaga;
yfir ok undir
stóðumk jötna vegir,
svá hætta ek höfði til.

translations.bellows:
Gunnloth gave on a golden stool
A drink of the marvelous mead;
A harsh reward did I let her have
For her heroic heart,
And her spirit troubled sore.
translations.bray:
It was Gunnlod who gave me on a golden throne
a draught of the glorious mead,
but with poor reward did I pay her back
for her true and troubled heart.

original:
Vel keypts litar hefi ek vel notit,
fás er fróðum vant,
því at Óðrerir
er nú upp kominn á alda vés jaðar.

translations.bellows:
The well-earned beauty well I enjoyed,
Little the wise man lacks;
So Othrörir now has up been brought
To the midst of the men of earth.

original:
Ifi er mér á, at ek væra enn kominn
jötna görðum ór,
ef ek Gunnlaðar né nytak,
innar góðu konu,
þeirar er lögðumk arm yfir.

translations.bellows:
Hardly, methinks, would I home have come,
And left the giants' land,
Had not Gunnloth helped me, the maiden good,
Whose arms about me had been.
translations.bray:
I misdoubt me if ever again I had come
from the realms of the Jotun race,
had I not served me of Gunnlod, sweet woman,
her whom I held in mine arms.

original:
Ins hindra dags gengu hrímþursar
Háva ráðs at fregna Háva höllu í;
at Bölverki þeir spurðu,
ef hann væri með böndum kominn
eða hefði hánum Suttungr of sóit.

translations.bellows:
The day that followed, the frost-giants came,
Some word of Hor to win,
(And into the hall of Hor;) Of Bolverk they asked, were he back midst the gods,
Or had Suttung slain him there?
translations.bray:
Came forth, next day, the dread Frost Giants,
and entered the High One's Hall:
they asked - was the Baleworker back mid the Powers,
or had Suttung slain him below?
translations.petit:
The following day, frost-giants went
to seek Hávi’s counsel in Hávi’s hall;
they asked about Bǫlverkr, whether he had come among the gods,
or whether Suttungr had slaughtered him.

original:
Baugeið Óðinn, hygg ek, at unnit hafi;
hvat skal hans tryggðum trúa?
Suttung svikinn hann lét sumbli frá
ok grætta Gunnlöðu.

translations.bellows:
On his ring swore Othin the oath, methinks;
Who now his troth shall trust?
Suttung's betrayal he sought with drink,
And Gunnloth to grief he left.
* * *
translations.bray:
A ring-oath Odin I trow had taken -
how shall one trust his troth?
it was he who stole the mead from Suttung,
and Gunnlod caused to weep.
translations.petit:
I think Óðinn has sworn a ring-oath;
how can his pledges be trusted?
He cheated Suttungr of the drink,
and caused Gunnlǫð to weep.

original:
Mál er at þylja þular stóli á
Urðarbrunni at, sá ek ok þagðak,
sá ek ok hugðak,
hlýdda ek á manna mál;
of rúnar heyrða ek dæma,
né of ráðum þögðu
Háva höllu at, Háva höllu í,
heyrða ek segja svá:

translations.bellows:
It is time to chant from the chanter's stool;
By the wells of Urth I was,
I saw and was silent, I saw and thought,
And heard the speech of Hor.
(Of runes heard I words, nor were counsels wanting,
At the hall of Hor,
In the hall of Hor;
Such was the speech I heard.)
translations.bray:
It is time to speak from the Sage's Seat;
hard by the Well of Weird
I saw and was silent, I saw and pondered,
I listened to the speech of men.
Of runes they spoke, and the reading of runes
was little withheld from their lips:
at the High One's hall, in the High One's hall,
I thus heard the High One say:

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
nótt þú rís-at nema á njósn séir
eða þú leitir þér innan út staðar.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Rise not at night, save if news you seek,
Or fain to the outhouse would fare.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
rise never at night time, except you are spying
or seekest a spot without.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, that you take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
don’t get up at night, unless you’re on watch
or looking, from inside, for a place to relieve yourself outside!

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
fjölkunnigri konu skal-at-tu í faðmi sofa,
svá at hon lyki þik liðum.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Beware of sleep on a witch's bosom,
Nor let her limbs ensnare you.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
you shall never sleep in the arms of a sorceress,
lest she should lock your limbs;
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, that you take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
you mustn’t sleep in the embrace of a woman wise in magic,
so that she locks you in her limbs.

original:
Hon svá gerir, at þú gáir eigi
þings né þjóðans máls;
mat þú vill-at né mannskis gaman,
ferr þú sorgafullr at sofa.

translations.bellows:
Such is her might that you have no mind
For the council or meeting of men;
Meat you hate, joy you have not,
And sadly to slumber you fare.
translations.bray:
So shall she charm that you shall not heed
the council, or words of the king,
nor care for your food, or the joys of mankind,
but fall into sorrowful sleep.
translations.petit:
Thus she’ll ensure you won’t pay attention
to the assembly or a ruler’s speech;
you won’t want food or the pleasure of anyone’s fellowship,
you’ll go to sleep sorrowful.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir,
en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr, þér munu góð,
ef þú getr -:
annars konu teygðu þér aldregi eyrarúnu at.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn: Seek never to win the wife of another,
Or long for her secret love.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
seek not ever to draw to yourself
in love-whispering another's wife.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, that you take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
never lure another’s wife
into being your lover.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir,
en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
á fjalli eða firði, ef þik fara tíðir,
fásktu at virði vel.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
If over mountains or gulfs you fain would go,
Look well to your food for the way.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
should you long to fare over fell and firth
provide you well with food.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir,
en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr, þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
illan mann láttu aldregi óhöpp at þér vita,
því at af illum manni fær þú aldregi
gjöld ins góða hugar.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
An evil man you must not let
Bring aught of ill to you;
For an evil man will never make
Reward for a worthy thought.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
tell not ever an evil man
if misfortunes you befall,
from such ill friend you need never seek
return for your trustful mind.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
never let a bad man
know your misfortunes,
because from a bad man you will never get
a reward for the good intention.

original:
Ofarla bíta ek sá einum hal
orð illrar konu;
fláráð tunga varð hánum at fjörlagi
ok þeygi of sanna sök.

translations.bellows:
I saw a man who was wounded sore
By an evil woman's word;
A lying tongue his death-blow launched,
And no word of truth there was.
translations.bray:
Wounded to death, have I seen a man
by the words of an evil woman;
a lying tongue had bereft him of life,
and all without reason of right.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
veistu, ef þú vin átt,
þann er þú vel trúir,
far þú at finna oft, því at hrísi vex
ok hávu grasi vegr, er vættki treðr.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
If a friend you have whom you fully will trust,
Then fare to find him oft;
For brambles grow and waving grass
On the rarely trodden road.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
have you a friend whom you trustest well,
fare you to find him often;
for with brushwood grows and with grasses high
the path were no foot passes.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
know [this, that] if you have a friend, one whom you trust well,
go to visit him often,
because a road that no one treads becomes overgrown
with brushwood and tall grass.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
góðan mann teygðu þér at gamanrúnum
ok nem líknargaldr, meðan þú lifir.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
A good man find to hold in friendship,
And give heed to his healing charms.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
in sweet converse call the righteous to your side,
learn a healing song while you live.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
draw a good man into a pleasing friendship with you,
and learn soothing charms while you live.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir,
en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
vin þínum ver þú aldregi fyrri at flaumslitum;
sorg etr hjarta, ef þú segja né náir
einhverjum allan hug.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,-
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Be never the first to break with your friend
The bond that holds you both;
Care eats the heart if you can not speak
To another all your thought.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
be never the first with friend of thine
to break the bond of fellowship;
care shall gnaw your heart if you cannot tell
all your mind to another.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
never be the first to sever
happy relations with your friend;
sorrow eats the heart if you can’t speak
your whole mind to someone.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
orðum skipta þú skalt aldregi
við ósvinna apa,

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Exchange of words with a witless ape
You must not ever make.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
never in speech with a foolish knave
should you waste a single word.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
you must never exchange words
with an unwise ape.

original:
Því at af illum manni mundu aldregi
góðs laun of geta,
en góðr maðr
mun þik gerva mega líknfastan at lofi.

translations.bellows:
For never you may from an evil man
A good requital get;
But a good man often the greatest love
Through words of praise will win you.
translations.bray:
From the lips of such you need not look
for reward of thine own good will;
but a righteous man by praise will render you
firm in favour and love.
translations.petit:
Because from a bad man you’ll never
get a reward for the goodwill,
but a good man can make you assured
of esteem by his praise.

translations.bellows:
Mingled is love when a man can speak
To another all his thought;
Nothing is so bad as false to be,
No friend speaks only fair.
translations.petit:
Kinship is exchanged when anyone decides
to tell his whole mind [to another];
anything is better than for one to be unreliable,
he who says only what’s agreeable is no friend to another.

original:
Ráðumk, þér Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr, þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
þrimr orðum senna skal-at-tu þér við verra mann
oft inn betri bilar,
þá er inn verri vegr.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
With a worse man speak not three words in dispute,
Ill fares the better oft
When the worse man wields a sword.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
often the worst lays the best one low.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir,
en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
skósmiðr þú verir né skeftismiðr,
nema þú sjalfum þér séir:
skór er skapaðr illa eða skaft sé rangt,
þá er þér böls beðit.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,-
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
A shoemaker be, or a maker of shafts,
For only your single self;
If the shoe is ill made, or the shaft prove false,
Then evil of you men think.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
be not a shoemaker nor yet a shaft maker
save for yourself alone:
let the shoe be misshapen, or crooked the shaft,
and a curse on your head will be called.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
be neither a shoe-maker nor a shaft-maker,
unless you be one for yourself;
[if] the shoe is badly made or the shaft is crooked,
then evil will be invoked for you.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir,
en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr, þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
hvars þú böl kannt, kveð þú þér bölvi at
ok gef-at þínum fjándum frið.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
If evil you know, as evil proclaim it,
And make no friendship with foes.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
when in peril you see you, confess you in peril,
nor ever give peace to your foes.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
wherever you perceive evil, declare it as evil,
and don’t give your enemies peace.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
illu feginn ver þú aldregi,
en lát þér at góðu getit.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
In evil never joy shall you know,
But glad the good shall make you.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
rejoice not ever at tidings of ill,
but glad let your soul be in good.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
never be gladdened by evil,
but let yourself be pleased with good.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
upp líta skal-at-tu í orrustu,
- gjalti glíkir verða gumna synir, -
síðr þitt of heilli halir.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Look not up when the battle is on,–
(Like madmen the sons of men become,–)
Lest men bewitch your wits.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
look not up in battle, when men are as beasts,
lest the wights bewitch you with spells.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
you mustn’t look up in battle —
the sons of men become like a madman —
lest men cast a spell on you.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
ef þú vilt þér góða konu kveðja at gamanrúnum
ok fá fögnuð af, fögru skaltu heita
ok láta fast vera;
leiðisk manngi gótt, ef getr.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,-
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
If you fain would win a woman's love,
And gladness get from her,
Fair be your promise and well fulfilled;
None loathes what good he gets.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
would you win joy of a gentle maiden,
and lure to whispering of love,
make fair promise, and let it be fast, -
none will scorn their weal who can win it.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
if you want to persuade a good woman into secret love with you
and get delight from her,
you must make fine promises and let them be firm:
no one loathes a good thing, if one gets it.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
varan bið ek þik vera ok eigi ofvaran;
ver þú við öl varastr ok við annars konu
ok við þat it þriðja, at þjófar né leiki.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,-
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
I bid you be wary, but be not fearful;
(Beware most with ale or another's wife,
And third beware lest a thief outwit you.)
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
I pray you be wary, yet not too wary,
be wariest of all with ale,
with another's wife, and a third thing eke,
that knaves outwit you never.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
at háði né hlátri hafðu aldregi
gest né ganganda.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,-
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Scorn or mocking never shall you make
Of a guest or a journey-goer.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
hold not in scorn, nor mock in your halls
a guest or wandering wight.
translations.petit:
I counsel you, Loddfáfnir, and you should take my counsels;
you’ll profit if you take them,
they’ll be good for you if you get them:
never treat a guest or a tramp
with scorn or derision.

original:
Oft vitu ógörla, þeir er sitja inni fyrir,
hvers þeir ro kyns, er koma;
er-at maðr svá góðr at galli né fylgi,
né svá illr, at einugi dugi.

translations.bellows:
Often scarcely he knows who sits in the house
What kind is the man who comes;
None so good is found that faults he has not,
Nor so wicked that nothing he is worth.
translations.bray:
They know but unsurely who sit within
what kind of man is come:
none is found so good, but some fault attends him,
or so ill but he serves for somewhat.
translations.petit:
Often those sitting inside are unsure
what kind of men are those who come;
no one is so good as to have no fault,
nor so bad as to be good for nothing.

original:
