Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
at hárum þul hlæ þú aldregi,
oft er gótt,
þat er gamlir kveða;
oft ór skörpum belg skilin orð koma
þeim er hangir með hám ok skollir með skrám
ok váfir með vílmögum.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Scorn not ever the gray-haired singer,
Often do the old speak good;
(Often from shrivelled skin come skillful counsels,
Though it hang with the hides,
And flap with the pelts,
And is blown with the bellies.)
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
hold never in scorn the hoary singer;
often the counsel of the old is good;
come words of wisdom from the withered lips
of him left to hang among hides,
to rock with the rennets
and swing with the skins.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr,
þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
gest þú né geyja né á grind hrekir;
get þú váluðum vel.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
Curse not your guest, nor show him your gate,
Deal well with a man in want.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
growl not at guests, nor drive them from the gate
but show yourself gentle to the poor.

original:
Rammt er þat tré, er ríða skal
öllum at upploki;
baug þú gef, eða þat biðja mun
þér læs hvers á liðu.

translations.bellows:
Strong is the beam that raised must be
To give an entrance to all;
Give it a ring, or grim will be
The wish it would work on you.
translations.bray:
Mighty is the bar to be moved away
for the entering in of all.
Shower your wealth, or men shall with you
every ill in your limbs.
translations.petit:
Strong is the beam that must slide
to open up for all;
give an arm-ring, or visitors will invoke
every kind of injury upon your limbs.

original:
Ráðumk þér, Loddfáfnir, en þú ráð nemir, -
njóta mundu, ef þú nemr, þér munu góð, ef þú getr -:
hvars þú öl drekkir, kjós þér jarðar megin,
því at jörð tekr við ölðri, en eldr við sóttum,
eik við abbindi, ax við fjölkynngi,
höll við hýrógi, -
heiftum skal mána kveðja, -
beiti við bitsóttum, en við bölvi rúnar,
fold skal við flóði taka.

translations.bellows:
I rede you, Loddfafnir! and hear my rede,–
Profit you have if you hear,
Great your gain if you learn:
When ale you drink) seek might of earth,
(For earth cures drink, and fire cures ills,
The oak cures tightness, the ear cures magic,
Rye cures rupture, the moon cures rage,
Grass cures the scab, and runes the sword-cut;)
The field absorbs the flood.
translations.bray:
I counsel you, Stray-Singer, accept my counsels,
they will be your boon if you obey them,
they will work your weal if you win them:
when ale you quaffest, call upon earth's might -
it is earth drinks in the floods.
Earth prevails over drink, but fire over sickness,
the oak over binding, the earcorn over witchcraft,
the rye spur over rupture, the moon over rages,
herb over cattle plagues, runes over harm.

original:
Veit ek, at ek hekk vindga meiði á
nætr allar níu, geiri undaðr
ok gefinn Óðni,
sjalfr sjalfum mér,
á þeim meiði, er manngi veit
hvers af rótum renn.
academic_note: Note: Bellows inserts a summary here; his Rúnatal begins at V139.

translations.bellows:
I ween that I hung on the windy tree,
Hung there for nights full nine;
With the spear I was wounded, and offered I was
To Othin, myself to myself,
On the tree that none may ever know
What root beneath it runs.
translations.bray:
I trow I hung on that windy Tree
nine whole days and nights,
stabbed with a spear, offered to Odin,
myself to mine own self given,
high on that Tree of which none has heard
from what roots it rises to heaven.
translations.petit:
I know that I hung on a windy tree
for all of nine nights,
wounded by a spear and given to Óðinn,
myself to myself,
on that tree of which no one knows
the kind of roots it runs from.

original:
Við hleifi mik
sældu né við hornigi;
nýsta ek niðr, nam ek upp rúnar,
æpandi nam, fell ek aftr þaðan.

translations.bellows:
None made me happy with loaf or horn,
And there below I looked;
I took up the runes, shrieking I took them,
And forthwith back I fell.
translations.bray:
None refreshed me ever with food or drink,
I peered right down in the deep;
crying aloud I lifted the Runes
then back I fell from thence.
translations.petit:
They blessed me with neither bread nor horn,
I peered down, I took up runes,
screaming I took them; I fell back from there.

original:
Fimbulljóð níu nam ek
af inum frægja syni Bölþorns, Bestlu föður,
ok ek drykk of gat
ins dýra mjaðar, ausinn Óðreri.

translations.bellows:
Nine mighty songs I got from the son
Of Bolthorn, Bestla's father;
And a drink I got of the goodly mead
Poured out from Othrörir.
translations.bray:
Nine mighty songs I learned from the great
son of Bale-thorn, Bestla's sire;
I drank a measure of the wondrous Mead,
with the Soulstirrer's drops I was showered.
translations.petit:
Nine mighty songs I learnt from the famous son
of Bǫlþórr, father of Bestla,
and I got a drink of the precious mead,
poured from Óðrerir.

original:
Þá nam ek frævask ok fróðr vera
ok vaxa ok vel hafask,
orð mér af orði
orðs leitaði, verk mér af verki
verks leitaði.

translations.bellows:
Then began I to thrive, and wisdom to get,
I grew and well I was;
Each word led me on to another word,
Each deed to another deed.
translations.bray:
Before long I bare fruit, and throve full well,
I grew and waxed in wisdom;
word following word, I found me words,
deed following deed, I wrought deeds.
translations.petit:
Then I began to produce seed and be fertile,
and to grow and feel well;
word sought word from my word,
deed sought deed from my deed.

original:
Rúnar munt þú finna ok ráðna stafi,
mjök stóra stafi, mjök stinna stafi,
er fáði fimbulþulr ok
gerðu ginnregin
ok reist hroftr rögna.

translations.bellows:
Runes shall you find, and fateful signs,
That the king of singers colored,
And the mighty gods have made;
Full strong the signs, full mighty the signs
That the ruler of gods doth write.
translations.bray:
Hidden Runes shall you seek and interpreted signs,
many symbols of might and power,
by the great Singer painted, by the high Powers fashioned,
graved by the Utterer of gods.
translations.petit:
Runes you will find and readable staves,
very great staves,
very stiff staves,
which Fimbulþulr coloured
and great powers created
and Hroptr of the ruling powers carved,

original:
Óðinn með ásum, en fyr alfum Dáinn,
Dvalinn ok dvergum fyrir,
Ásviðr jötnum fyrir,
ek reist sjalfr sumar.

translations.bellows:
Othin for the gods, Dain for the elves,
And Dvalin for the dwarfs,
Alsvith for giants and all mankind,
And some myself I wrote.
translations.bray:
For gods graved Odin, for elves graved Dan,
Dvalin the Dallier for dwarfs,
All-wise for Jotuns, and I, of myself,
graved some for the sons of men.
translations.petit:
Óðinn among the Æsir, and Dáinn for the elves,
Dvalinn for the dwarves,
Ásviðr for the giants,
I carved some myself.

original:
Veistu, hvé rísta skal? Veistu, hvé ráða skal?
Veistu, hvé fáa skal? Veistu, hvé freista skal?
Veistu, hvé biðja skal? Veistu, hvé blóta skal?
Veistu, hvé senda skal? Veistu, hvé sóa skal?

translations.bellows:
Know how one shall write, know how one shall rede?
Know how one shall tint, know how one makes trial?
Know how one shall ask, know how one shall offer?
Know how one shall send, know how one shall sacrifice?
translations.bray:
Do you now how to write, know how to read,
know how to paint, know how to prove,
know how to ask, know how to offer,
know how to send, know how to spend?
translations.petit:
Do you know how one must carve? Do you know how one must read?
Do you know how one must colour? Do you know how one must test?
Do you know how one must invoke? Do you know how one must offer?
Do you know how one must send? Do you know how one must immolate?

original:
Betra er óbeðit en sé ofblótit,
ey sér til gildis gjöf;
betra er ósent en sé ofsóit.
Svá Þundr of reist fyr þjóða rök,
þar hann upp of reis, er hann aftr of kom.

translations.bellows:
Better no prayer than too big an offering,
By your getting measure your gift;
Better is none than too big a sacrifice,
. . .
So Thund of old wrote before man's race began,
Where he rose on high when home he came.
* * *

original:
Ljóð ek þau kann,
er kann-at þjóðans kona
ok mannskis mögr.
Hjalp heitir eitt,en þat þér hjalpa mun
við sökum ok sorgum ok sútum görvöllum.

translations.bellows:
The songs I know that king's wives know not,
Nor men that are sons of men;
The first is called help, and help it can bring you
In sorrow and pain and sickness.
translations.bray:
Those songs I know, which nor sons of men
nor queen in a king's court knows;
the first is Help which will bring you help
in all woes and in sorrow and strife.
translations.petit:
I know those songs that a ruler’s wife doesn’t know,
and no one’s son;
one is called ‘help’, and it will help you
against disputes and sorrows, and each and every suffering.

original:
Þat kann ek annat,
er þurfu ýta synir,
þeir er vilja læknar lifa.

translations.bellows:
A second I know, that men shall need
Who leechcraft long to use;
translations.bray:
A second I know, which the son of men
must sing, who would heal the sick.
translations.petit:
I know it, a second, which the sons of men need,
those who want to live as healers.

original:
Það kann ek þriðja:
ef mér verðr þörf mikil hafts við mína heiftmögu,
eggjar ek deyfi minna andskota,
bíta-t þeim vápn né velir.

translations.bellows:
A third I know, if great is my need
Of fetters to hold my foe;
Blunt do I make my enemy's blade,
Nor bites his sword or staff.
translations.bray:
A third I know: if sore need should come
of a spell to stay my foes;
when I sing that song, which shall blunt their swords,
nor their weapons nor staves can wound.
translations.petit:
I know it, the third: if I have great need
of a fetter for my foes,
I blunt the edges of my enemies;
for them neither weapons nor staffs will bite.

original:
Þat kann ek it fjórða:
ef mér fyrðar bera bönd að boglimum,
svá ek gel,
at ek ganga má,
sprettr mér af fótum fjöturr, en af höndum haft.

translations.bellows:
A fourth I know, if men shall fasten
Bonds on my bended legs;
So great is the charm that forth I may go,
The fetters spring from my feet,
Broken the bonds from my hands.
translations.bray:
A fourth I know: if men make fast
in chains the joints of my limbs,
when I sing that song which shall set me free,
spring the fetters from hands and feet.
translations.petit:
I know it, the fourth: if people put
bonds on my arms and legs,
I chant so that I can walk;
the fetter springs from my legs,
and the shackle from my arms.

original:
Þat kann ek it fimmta:
ef ek sé af fári skotinn flein í folki vaða, fýgr-a hann svá stinnt, at ek stöðvig-a-k, ef ek hann sjónum of sék.

translations.bellows:
A fifth I know, if I see from afar
An arrow fly 'gainst the folk;
It flies not so swift that I stop it not,
If ever my eyes behold it.
translations.bray:
A fifth I know: when I see, by foes shot,
speeding a shaft through the host,
flies it never so strongly I still can stay it,
if I get but a glimpse of its flight.
translations.petit:
I know it, the fifth: if I see a shaft shot
in malice flying in battle,
it doesn’t fly so forcefully that I can’t stop it,
if I see it with my sight.

original:
Þat kann ek it sétta:
ef mik særir þegn á vrótum hrás viðar,
ok þann hal er mik heifta kveðr,
þann eta mein heldr en mik.

translations.bellows:
A sixth I know, if harm one seeks
With a sapling's roots to send me;
The hero himself who wreaks his hate
Shall taste the ill before I.
translations.bray:
A sixth I know: when some thane would harm me
in runes on a moist tree's root,
on his head alone shall light the ills
of the curse that he called upon mine.
translations.petit:
I know it, the sixth: if a warrior wounds me
on the roots of a strong tree —
so too with that man who provokes my hatred —
harm will devour him, rather than me.

original:
Þat kann ek it sjaunda:
ef ek sé hávan loga sal of sessmögum,
brennr-at svá breitt, at ek hánum bjargig-a-k;
þann kann ek galdr at gala.

translations.bellows:
A seventh I know, if I see in flames
The hall over my comrades' heads;
It burns not so wide that I will not quench it,
I know that song to sing.
translations.bray:
A seventh I know: if I see a hall
high over the bench-mates blazing,
flame it never so fiercely I still can save it, -
I know how to sing that song.
translations.petit:
I know it, the seventh: if I see a high hall
blazing around bench-mates,
it doesn’t burn so broadly that I can’t save it;
I know the incantation to chant.

original:
Þat kann ek it átta, er öllum er
nytsamligt at nema:
hvars hatr vex með hildings sonum
þat má ek bæta brátt.

translations.bellows:
An eighth I know, that is to all
Of greatest good to learn;
When hatred grows among heroes' sons,
I soon can set it right.
translations.bray:
An eighth I know: which all can sing
for their weal if they learn it well;
where hate shall wax 'mid the warrior sons,
I can calm it soon with that song.
translations.petit:
I know it, the eighth, which is useful
for all to learn:
wherever hatred grows between a warrior’s sons,
I can swiftly settle it.

original:
Þat kann ek it níunda:
ef mik nauðr of stendr at
bjarga fari mínu á floti,
vind ek kyrri vági á ok svæfik allan sæ.

translations.bellows:
A ninth I know, if need there comes
To shelter my ship on the flood;
The wind I calm upon the waves,
And the sea I put to sleep.
translations.bray:
A ninth I know: when need befalls me
to save my vessel afloat,
I hush the wind on the stormy wave,
and soothe all the sea to rest.
translations.petit:
I know it, the ninth: if need arises for me
to save my ship when it’s afloat,
I calm the wind on the wave
and lull all the sea to sleep.

original:
Þat kann ek it tíunda:
ef ek sé túnriður leika lofti á,
ek svá vinnk, at þær villar fara
sinna heimhama, sinna heimhuga.

translations.bellows:
A tenth I know, what time I see
House-riders flying on high;
So can I work that wildly they go,
Showing their true shapes,
Hence to their own homes.
translations.bray:
A tenth I know: when at night the witches
ride and sport in the air,
such spells I weave that they wander home
out of skins and wits bewildered.
translations.petit:
I know it, the tenth: if I see fence-riders
sporting in the air,
I bring it about that they go astray
to the home of their shapes(?),
to the home of their minds(?).

original:
Þat kann ek it ellifta:
ef ek skal til orrostu leiða langvini,
und randir ek gel,
en þeir með ríki fara
heilir hildar til, heilir hildi frá,
koma þeir heilir hvaðan.

translations.bellows:
An eleventh I know, if needs I must lead
To the fight my long-loved friends;
I sing in the shields, and in strength they go
Whole to the field of fight,
Whole from the field of fight,
And whole they come thence home.
translations.bray:
An eleventh I know: if haply I lead
my old comrades out to war,
I sing 'neath the shields, and they fare forth mightily
safe into battle,
safe out of battle,
and safe return from the strife.
translations.petit:
I know it, the eleventh: if I have to lead
long-standing friends into conflict,
I chant beneath the shield-rims, and they journey with strength,
safe to battle,
safe from battle,
they come safe from anywhere.

original:
Þat kann ek it tolfta:
ef ek sé á tré
uppi váfa virgilná,
svá ek ríst ok í rúnum fák,
at sá gengr gumi ok mælir við mik.

translations.bellows:
A twelfth I know, if high on a tree
I see a hanged man swing; So do I write and color the runes
That forth he fares,
And to me talks.
translations.bray:
A twelfth I know: if I see in a tree
a corpse from a halter hanging,
such spells I write, and paint in runes,
that the being descends and speaks.
translations.petit:
I know it, the twelfth: if I see a halter-corpse
dangling up in a tree,
I so carve and colour-in runes
that that man walks
and talks with me.

original:
Þat kann ek it þrettánda:
ef ek skal þegn ungan verpa vatni á,
mun-at hann falla, þótt hann í folk komi,
hnígr-a sá halr fyr hjörum.

translations.bellows:
A thirteenth I know, if a thane full young
With water I sprinkle well;
He shall not fall, though he fares mid the host,
Nor sink beneath the swords.
translations.bray:
A thirteenth I know: if the new-born son
of a warrior I sprinkle with water,
that youth will not fail when he fares to war,
never slain show he bow before sword.
translations.petit:
I know it, the thirteenth: if I have to throw
water on a young warrior,
he won’t fall, even if he comes into battle,
that man won’t sink before swords.

original:
Þat kann ek it fjögurtánda:
ef ek skal fyrða liði telja tíva fyrir,
ása ok alfa ek kann allra skil;
fár kann ósnotr svá.

translations.bellows:
A fourteenth I know, if fain I would name
To men the mighty gods;
All know I well of the gods and elves,
Few be the fools know this.
translations.bray:
A fourteenth I know: if I needs must number
the Powers to the people of men,
I know all the nature of gods and of elves
which none can know untaught.
translations.petit:
I know it, the fourteenth: if I have to count the gods
before a company of people,
of Æsir and elves I know all their attributes;
few fools can do that.

original:
Þat kann ek it fimmtánda
er gól Þjóðrerir dvergr fyr Dellings durum:
afl gól hann ásum,en alfum frama,
hyggju Hroftatý.

translations.bellows:
A fifteenth I know, that before the doors
Of Delling sang Thjothrörir the dwarf;
Might he sang for the gods, and glory for elves,
And wisdom for Hroptatyr wise.
translations.bray:
A fifteenth I know, which Folk-stirrer sang,
the dwarf, at the gates of Dawn;
he sang strength to the gods, and skill to the elves,
and wisdom to Odin who utters.
translations.petit:
I know it, the fifteenth, which Þjóðreyrir, a dwarf,
chanted before Dellingr’s doors:
he chanted strength to the Æsir, and courage to the elves,
intellect to Hroptatýr.

original:
Þat kann ek it sextánda:
ef ek vil ins svinna mans hafa
geð allt ok gaman, hugi ek hverfi
hvítarmri konu, ok sný ek hennar öllum sefa.

translations.bellows:
A sixteenth I know, if I seek delight
To win from a maiden wise;
The mind I turn of the white-armed maid,
And thus change all her thoughts.
translations.bray:
A sixteenth I know: when all sweetness and love
I would win from some artful wench,
her heart I turn, and the whole mind change
of that fair-armed lady I love.
translations.petit:
I know it, the sixteenth: if I wish to have the whole heart
and sexual pleasure of the wise girl,
I turn the feelings of the white-armed woman
and I twist her whole heart.

original:
Þat kann ek it sjautjánda
at mik mun seint firrask
it manunga man.
Ljóða þessa mun þú, Loddfáfnir,
lengi vanr vera;
þó sé þér góð, ef þú getr,
nýt ef þú nemr, þörf ef þú þiggr.

translations.bellows:
A seventeenth I know, so that seldom shall go
A maiden young from me;
translations.bray:
A seventeenth I know: so that even the shy maiden
is slow to shun my love.
translations.petit:
I know it, the seventeenth, so that the girly girl
will be slow to leave me.
These songs, Loddfáfnir, you will
long be lacking,
though it would be good for you if you get them,
[they would be] useful if you take them,
needful if you receive them.

original:
Þat kann ek it átjánda,
er ek æva kennik mey né manns konu,
- allt er betra, er einn of kann;
þat fylgir ljóða lokum, -
nema þeiri einni, er mik armi verr,
eða mín systir sé.

translations.bellows:
An eighteenth I know, that never will I tell
To maiden or wife of man,–
The best is what none but one's self doth know,
So comes the end of the songs,–
Save only to her in whose arms I lie,
Or who else my sister is.
translations.bray:
An eighteenth I know: which I never shall tell
to maiden or wife of man
save alone to my sister, or haply to her
who folds me fast in her arms;
most safe are secrets known to but one-
the songs are sung to an end.

original:
Nú eru Háva mál kveðin Háva höllu í,
allþörf ýta sonum, óþörf jötna sonum;
heill sá, er kvað, heill sá, er kann,
njóti sá, er nam, heilir, þeirs hlýddu.
academic_note: Conclusion: This summary stanza is placed at the end of the original manuscript.

translations.bellows:
Now are Hor's words spoken in the hall,
Kind for the kindred of men,
Cursed for the kindred of giants:
Hail to the speaker, and to him who learns!
Profit be his who has them!
Hail to them who hearken!
* * *
translations.bray:
Now the sayings of the High One are uttered in the hall
for the weal of men, for the woe of Jotuns,
Hail, you who have spoken! Hail, you that knowest!
Hail, you that have hearkened! Use, you who have learned!
translations.petit:
Now Hávi’s sayings have been spoken in Hávi’s hall,
most useful to the sons of men,
useless to the sons of men(?)!260
Hail to the one who spoke! Hail to the one who knows!
Let the one who learned profit!261
Hail to those who listened!
